verdict: 2026-07-19 07:03:00 PDT — The overall shape is sound: a separate plan-only skill, detection reused by reference, declarative steering, drift-only linting, existing wiring conventions, and a loud terminal stop all fit OPS-13 and this repository. It is not ready to build, however, because the proposed “compiler” does not yet define deterministic rule resolution or a canonical machine-readable plan contract, and its priced consult is not represented in the plan rows themselves.

findings.title: The suggestion matrix is declarative in name but not deterministic in operation
findings.locus: Design sketch — HOW / Steps 2–3 and acceptance criterion 4
findings.claim: The sketch promises that the same detected profile always produces the same suggestion, but its inputs and resolution rules remain judgment calls: `git log --since` has no fixed window; “weak tests,” “large,” “recent large refactor,” “small mature repo,” and “never-reviewed mass” have no predicates or thresholds; overlapping signals can propose conflicting depths for the same lens/altitude/scope; and “told” overrides have neither a declared syntax nor replacement key. An agent following this prose can therefore compile different plans from the same repository, violating the central compiled-plan invariant that the later engine will inherit.
findings.alternative: Define one normalized rule table with exact signal predicates and thresholds, a stable plan-row identity such as `(lens, altitude, scope)`, an explicit collision rule such as highest depth wins with accumulated reasons, a fixed churn window, and a small normalized override grammar for include/exclude/depth changes. Keep all profile-to-plan decisions in that construct.
findings.win: Eliminates per-run judgment from suggestion compilation, makes AC4 testable by inspection, and gives the future engine one stable steering rule instead of inheriting ambiguous prompt behavior.

findings.title: The JSON ledger stub has no canonical schema or source of truth
findings.locus: Open question 3; Design sketch — HOW / Step 4 Compile
findings.claim: “Markdown plus a JSON companion, compiled together” is not yet a design for consistency: the only compiler is an instruction-following agent, the JSON’s stable keys and version are unspecified, and neither artifact is declared canonical. Since OPS-13 says the engine will execute this JSON and differential mode will extend it, this is a one-way data contract rather than a disposable companion file. Independent dual writing can produce an approved Markdown plan that differs from the machine-consumed JSON without anything detecting the divergence.
findings.alternative: Ratify JSON as the canonical, versioned plan artifact now, define its required fields and row identity in a JSON Schema, and derive or mechanically validate the Markdown consult projection from that same data. If that contract is intentionally deferred, ship Markdown only and stop calling this slice the C-ledger layout stub.
findings.win: Creates one authoritative approved plan, prevents human-view/engine-view drift, and avoids forcing the engine story to reverse-engineer or replace an already-shipped artifact contract.

findings.title: The plan artifact does not contain the priced tradeoffs the consult requires
findings.locus: Acceptance criterion 5; Open question 4; Design sketch — HOW / Steps 4–5
findings.claim: Plan rows contain only lens, altitude, scope, depth, and why, while the mandatory consult requires every row to state cost and risk. That forces a second, ad hoc presentation outside the artifact Thomas edits and approves. The proposed `units × plan rows` arithmetic is also inaccurate for scoped rows: not every row applies to every unit, depths have different costs, and wall-clock time depends on batches at the concurrency cap rather than a single Cartesian product.
findings.alternative: Make each canonical plan row carry its resolved unit count, run count, depth-specific token assumption, marginal cost, and inclusion/omission risk. Compute totals as the sum of scoped rows and wall-clock order from concurrency-bounded batches; present those same stored rows at the consult.
findings.win: Centralizes approval and pricing in one artifact, removes a parallel narrative that can drift from the plan, and produces cost estimates tied to the work the future engine will actually schedule.
